quiz.title: Unit 3 Quiz 5: Cost-Push Inflation, Aggregate Demand, and Macroeconomic Equilibrium
quiz.subtitle: Cost-Push Triggers, Desirability of Inflation/Disinflation, AD Components, and AE = GDP
quiz.subject: Macroeconomics
quiz.topic: Cost-Push Inflation, Aggregate Demand, Aggregate Expenditure, Macroeconomic Equilibrium

questions.question: SELECT ALL THAT APPLY: This can lead to cost-push inflation
options: Significant spike in global energy prices
Imported capital goods seeing higher costs
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation is caused by increases in production costs that shift the SRAS curve leftward. Energy prices (A), nominal wages (B), imported capital goods costs (C), and raw material scarcity (D) all raise production costs. Raising the retirement age (E) increases labor supply, putting downward pressure on wages — this reduces cost-push pressure. An increase in interest rates (F) affects aggregate demand, not production costs directly.

questions.question: Which of the following may be considered desirable? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
questions.explanation: Disinflation (C) is a decrease in the inflation rate — desirable when an economy is reducing high inflation to a stable, low level without entering deflation. Inflation (A) erodes purchasing power and creates uncertainty. Deflation (B) leads to delayed spending and increased real debt burdens. Recession (D) and depression (E) involve falling output, rising unemployment, and economic hardship.

questions.question: What constitutes aggregate demand? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
options: Total sum of demand in an industry
Total sum of demand in an economy
It is equal to the aggregate income
It is equal to the aggregate expenditure
Comprises only demand for commodities, not income
questions.explanation: Aggregate demand is the total demand for all final goods and services in an entire economy (B). In macroeconomic equilibrium, aggregate demand equals aggregate output, which equals aggregate income (C) and aggregate expenditure (D). Option A is microeconomic, not macroeconomic. Option E is false because AD and aggregate income are conceptually equal in the circular flow model.

questions.question: When aggregate expenditure equals to GDP, what does it mean? SELECT ALL THAT APPLY
options: Firms may or may not have inventories
Consumers buy all the units that are produced
questions.explanation: When AE = GDP, the economy is in short-run equilibrium. Firms may or may not hold inventories (A) — the key is that inventories are unchanged. Output remains constant (E) because firms have no immediate reason to change production. All output is purchased (F) — that's the definition of AE = GDP. The economy is not necessarily at potential GDP (B). Saving (C) and net exports (D) can be positive, negative, or zero.

questions.question: An economy is at potential GDP when it experiences a sudden increase in production costs. As a result, the economy experiences _______.
options: A. A rise in the price level and an increase in real GDP
B. A rise in the price level and a decrease in potential GDP
C. A fall in the price level and a decrease in real GDP
questions.explanation: An increase in production costs shifts the SRAS curve to the left. This causes the price level to rise (inflation) while real GDP falls below potential (stagnation). The simultaneous occurrence of these two events is known as stagflation.

scoreMessages.100: Perfect! You understand cost-push inflation, aggregate demand concepts, and macroeconomic equilibrium.
scoreMessages.80: Excellent! Review the difference between disinflation, deflation, and inflation.
scoreMessages.60: Good work. Focus on what AE = GDP really means for inventories and output.
scoreMessages.40: Keep studying. Pay attention to which factors affect SRAS vs AD.
scoreMessages.0: Review: Cost-push starts with production costs. AD = total economy-wide demand. AE = GDP means short-run equilibrium.
